Comparison of the supports of X̃ , X ′, and X̃ (with P = 0.04) in the unit-circle example for 1-bit quantization with 1-bit of common randomness (M = 2, N = 2) and 2-bit quantization with no common randomness (M = 4, N = 1). Fig. 2. Due to the perception constraint, pX̂ must lie within the Wasserstein-2 ball of radius √ P centered at pZ . Among all such pẐ , the one that minimizes W2(pẐ , pZ̃) is located at the point where the geodesic from pZ to pZ̃ intersects the boundary of this ball. For this choice of pẐ , we have W2(pẐ , pZ̃) = W2(pZ , pZ̃)− √ P .

Fig. 11. Consider a random vector Z in Rk with E[∥Z∥2] < ∞, and a representation W generated according to some conditional distribution pW |Z . Let Z̃ be the MMSE estimate of Z based on W , and let Ẑ be a reconstruction derived from W . For a given perception measure Ξ, the constraint Ξ(pZ , pẐ) ≤ P defines a perceptually permissible set (illustrated as an elliptical region) within which pẐ must lie. The interpolation scheme selects pẐ as the point where the Wasserstein-2 geodesic from pZ to pZ̃ intersects the boundary of this set. For this choice, W2(pZ , pẐ) = √ P ′, where P ′ may differ from P when Ξ is not the squared Wasserstein-2 distance. However, this choice is generally not optimal in minimizing W2(pẐ , pZ̃) over the perceptually permissible set (as indicated by the starred point). By instead generating Ẑ based on Z̃ via a Wasserstein-2 optimal transport plan that maps pZ̃ to this optimal point, one obtains a smaller value of W2(pẐ , pZ̃), and consequently a lower end-to-end distortion E[∥Z − Ẑ∥2]. See Fig. 2 for a comparison.
Fig. 6. Comparison of the distortion–perception tradeoffs in the unit-circle example for 1-bit quantization with no common randomness, 1-bit of common randomness, and unlimited common randomness.

Fig. 5. Illustration of the supports of X̃ , X ′, and X̂ (with P = 0.04) in the unit-circle example for 1-bit quantization with unlimited common randomness. In this case, X̃ , X ′, and X̂ are uniformly distributed over circles centered at the origin with radii 2 π ≈ 0.637, 1, and 0.8, respectively, and are deterministically related through scaling.
Fig. 9. System diagram of perception-aware lossy source coding.

Fig. 10. Illustration of a lattice-based construction. The blue points represent the intermediate lattice Λ2, while the yellow and red points denote its cosets; together, they form the finest lattice Λ3. The Voronoi regions of points in Λ2 (or its cosets) are delineated by solid lines in the corresponding colors and are associated with operational encoding, which essentially amounts to conventional lattice quantization, mapping each region to its lattice point; the choice of coset depends on the realization of the random seed K. The Voronoi regions of points in the finest lattice Λ3, shown with dashed lines, are associated with virtual encoding, whose stochastic inverse is used to generate perceptually perfect samples. The shaded part represents the fundamental Voronoi region V1 of the shaping lattice Λ1. Bit representations are restricted to points in Λ2 (or its cosets) that lie within V1.

Fig. 3. Illustration of the supports of X̂ across different values of P in the unit-circle example for 1-bit quantization. Note that X̂ coincides with X ′ when P = 0, and with X̃ when P → ∞ (more precisely, when P ≥ 1− 4 π2 ≈ 0.595).
